does: This paper proposes a unified Pair-GRPO framework for LLM alignment through pairwise preference-based reinforcement learning, introducing two variants: Soft-Pair-GRPO and Hard-Pair-GRPO. Soft-Pair-GRPO modifies Group Relative Policy Optimization by replacing scalar group rewards with binary pairwise preferences while preserving GRPO's clipped surrogate and KL regularization structure. The authors prove a gradient equivalence theorem showing that Soft-Pair-GRPO's gradient is a positive scalar multiple of GRPO's gradient under first-order Taylor expansion, explaining its empirical stability despite losing continuous reward information. Hard-Pair-GRPO extends this framework with more sophisticated preference constraint handling, offering improved gradient clarity and lower variance in pairwise preference learning scenarios.
connects: This work builds directly on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) and the broader landscape of RLHF methods for LLM alignment, addressing known instabilities in existing pairwise preference learning paradigms. The paper responds to longstanding challenges in preference-based RL including unstable policy updates, gradient ambiguity, and high variance that plague mainstream methods like RLHF. It connects to theoretical work on policy optimization algorithms and preference learning, bridging implicit preference representations (through reward models) and explicit preference constraints (direct pairwise comparisons). The framework inherits from and builds upon advances in reward-robust policy optimization and KL-regularized training approaches that have become standard in modern LLM alignment practices.
enables: This framework enables more stable and interpretable LLM alignment through explicit preference-based optimization with proven gradient properties. The gradient equivalence theorem provides theoretical grounding for why binary preference signals can be effective alternatives to learned reward models, potentially enabling simpler, more direct alignment pipelines without intermediate reward modeling steps. The distinction between implicit and explicit preference constraints opens pathways for developing hybrid approaches that combine strengths of both paradigms. Future work can leverage these insights to build alignment methods with better sample efficiency, reduced training instability, and improved generalization across diverse preference types. The unified framework also suggests opportunities for analyzing other preference-learning variants and potentially extending these techniques to multi-agent settings or more complex preference structures beyond pairwise comparisons.
